mnemonics.title: E-Waste Categories
mnemonics.content: LIGHTS: Large appliances, IT/Telecom, Games/Toys, Household, Tools, Health/Medical, Small appliances

mnemonics.title: Hazardous Elements
mnemonics.content: Lead (L) Mercury (M) Cadmium (C) - LMC: Leaches into Memory/Creates Damage to brain/kidneys/neurological system

mnemonics.title: 3R Principle
mnemonics.content: R3: Reduce (don't buy excess) → Reuse (use again) → Recycle (process materials) - Think: 'Reduce Reuse Recycle' for a cleaner planet

mnemonics.title: Recycling Steps
mnemonics.content: CBSD: Collection → Sorting → Dismantling → Shredding → Dissolution/Recovery

acronyms.usage: Policy where manufacturers responsible for end-of-life management of their products
Old TV/computer monitors containing leaded glass
Ozone-depleting gases found in cooling equipment
Nodal agency for e-waste management in India
Toxic chemicals that persist in environment from e-waste

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flow diagram: Collection → Sorting → Dismantling → Shredding → Magnetic Separation (iron) → Eddy Current (non-ferrous) → Chemical Processing → Material Recovery → Safe Disposal
Create a simple device diagram showing: Batteries (Cd, Li), PCB (Pb, Hg), Screen (Hg), Cables (PVC), Capacitors (PCBs) - Color code: Red = highly toxic, Yellow = toxic, Green = recoverable
Pyramid shape: Top (Reduce) = Most effective, Middle (Reuse), Bottom (Recycle) - Each level decreases in environmental benefit
Circle diagram with: Producers (design, EPR), Consumers (dispose properly), Recyclers (safe processing), Government (regulations), Informal sector (collection) - All interconnected

analogies.analogy: Like a hospital for electronics - doctors (recyclers) diagnose (sort), operate (dismantle), treat (recycle), and either cure (reuse) or safely dispose (incinerate/landfill) the patient (e-waste)
Like panning for gold in electronic rivers - just as miners sift through river sediment for precious metals, urban miners sift through e-waste for gold, silver, copper
Like a car manufacturer responsible for car's entire lifecycle - must take back old cars and ensure proper recycling, not just sell new ones
Our homes and offices are like mines - each device contains precious metals worth more than the device itself if properly recovered
